title: Eye Injuries: Spot It, Don't Touch It, Get Help
summary: Learn how to notice a serious eye injury during a game or practice and why you should never touch, rub, or press on it yourself.

sections.heading: Why eyes need special care
body: A ball, elbow, or finger to the eye can happen fast during basketball, baseball, soccer, or almost any sport. Most of the time it's just a bump that hurts for a minute and then feels fine.

But sometimes a hit to the eye is a real emergency. Your eye is soft and delicate, and the wrong move — like rubbing it or pressing on it — can make an injury much worse, even if you're trying to help. Your job is not to fix an eye injury. Your job is to notice it and get an adult right away.

sections.heading: Signs something is seriously wrong
body: After a hit near the eye, ask the athlete these questions and look at the eye. Any one of these means it's serious:
list: Vision looks blurry, doubled, or is missing in that eye
The eye hurts a lot and the pain isn't going away
The athlete can't open the eye normally, or the eyelid is cut
The eye looks different from the other eye — swollen, sunken in, or an odd shape
You can see blood in the white part of the eye
Something is stuck in or sticking out of the eye

image.description: Side-by-side illustration comparing a normal eye and an injured eye, with the injured eye showing mild redness in the white part and slight swelling around it. Simple, clean, non-graphic style appropriate for middle schoolers, minimal labels.
image.alt: Comparison drawing of a normal eye next to an injured eye with redness and swelling.

callout.title: See any of these signs? Stop and get an adult now
callout.text: Do not wait to see if it gets better on its own. Any change in vision, an eye that looks different from the other one, blood in the eye, or something stuck in the eye means the athlete needs to see a doctor right away.

sections.heading: What to do right away
body: Get a coach, athletic trainer, or other trusted adult immediately — this is not something a student should handle alone.
While waiting for the adult, keep the athlete calm and sitting or lying still. Do not let them keep playing.
list: Never try to pull out or remove anything stuck in the eye
Never try to force the eyelid open if it won't open

image.description: Simple icon-style illustration of a hand held near an eye with a red circle-and-slash 'no' symbol overlaid, showing 'do not touch or rub the eye.' Plain, flat icon style, minimal detail, no text in the image.
image.alt: Icon showing a hand near an eye with a red no symbol, meaning do not touch the eye.

callout.title: Never touch an injured eye
callout.text: Rubbing, pressing, or poking at an injured eye — even gently — can make the damage much worse. If something is stuck in the eye, leave it exactly where it is and get an adult.

sections.heading: When it's just something minor
body: Sometimes dust, an eyelash, or a bit of dirt blows into the eye and it just feels scratchy or waters a little. There was no hit, and vision is normal.

In that case, blinking a lot can help tears wash it out on their own. An adult can also gently rinse the eye with clean water. If it still feels scratchy after that, or if it starts to hurt more, tell an adult — it's time for a doctor to look at it, not to keep trying to fix it yourself.

image.description: Simple, calm illustration of an adult gently rinsing an athlete's eye with clean water poured from a small cup, the athlete leaning slightly forward and tilting their head. Clean, plain illustration style for middle schoolers.
image.alt: An adult gently rinsing an athlete's eye with clean water from a small cup.

sections.heading: Prevention: wear the right eyewear
body: The best way to deal with a serious eye injury is to never get one. Sports like racquetball, hockey, and lacrosse require athletes to wear protective eyewear made for that sport, and for good reason — a ball or stick can hit the eye faster than you can blink away.

Regular glasses or sunglasses are not the same thing. Sports eyewear is built to not shatter and to keep objects away from your eye. If your sport has protective eyewear, wear it every time, not just when you remember.

image.description: Simple front-facing illustration of an athlete wearing sport-specific protective eyewear, like wraparound goggles suited for a sport such as hockey or racquetball. Clean, flat, friendly illustration style.
image.alt: Athlete wearing sport-specific protective wraparound eyewear.

quiz.question: A teammate gets hit in the eye with an elbow during a rebound. What is your job?
choices: Try to fix the eye yourself so play can continue
Tell them to blink it off and keep playing
Wait a few minutes to see if it gets better before doing anything
Notice something is wrong and get an adult right away
quiz.explanation: A middle school athlete's job is to recognize a possible eye injury and get a trusted adult immediately — not to treat it or decide if it's serious enough on their own.

quiz.question: Which of these is a sign that an eye injury is serious?
choices: The athlete blinks a couple of times after the hit
Vision in that eye is blurry or missing
The eye waters a little
The athlete says the hit surprised them
quiz.explanation: A vision change like blurriness or loss of sight in the injured eye is a red-flag sign of a serious injury. Blinking or a little watering right after a bump is common and not, by itself, a warning sign.

quiz.question: An athlete's eye looks swollen and shaped differently than their other eye after getting hit with a ball. What should you do?
choices: Press on it gently to check how bad it is
Ignore it since there's no blood
Get an adult immediately
Tell the athlete to rub it to make it feel normal again
quiz.explanation: An eye that looks different from the other eye is a warning sign even without visible blood. Get an adult right away — never press on or rub an injured eye.

quiz.question: Why should you never rub or press on an eye after it's been hit?
choices: It could make the injury much worse
It will smear the athlete's eye makeup
It's against the rules of most sports
It has no effect either way, so it doesn't matter
quiz.explanation: Even gentle rubbing or pressure on an injured eye can make the damage worse. This is why hands-off is the rule until a trained adult or doctor takes over.

quiz.question: Something is sticking out of an athlete's eye after a fall. What should you do?
choices: Leave it alone and get an adult immediately
Carefully pull it out so it stops moving around
Wipe it with a towel
Tape it down tightly with gauze
quiz.explanation: Never touch or try to remove anything stuck in an eye — leave it exactly where it is and get an adult or emergency help right away. Pulling it out yourself can cause serious damage.

quiz.question: A little dirt blows into a soccer player's eye during a drill. There was no hit, and her vision is normal, but it feels scratchy. What is a reasonable first step?
choices: Rub the eye firmly until the dirt comes out
Immediately call 911
Cover the eye with a rigid shield and tape
Have her blink and let an adult gently rinse the eye with clean water
quiz.explanation: Loose dirt or dust without a hit or vision change is usually minor. Blinking and a gentle rinse with clean water can help — rubbing can scratch the eye and make it worse.

quiz.question: After rinsing, the scratchy feeling in an athlete's eye doesn't go away and starts to hurt more. What should happen next?
choices: Keep rinsing on your own until it stops hurting
Have the athlete rub it to work the dirt loose
Tell an adult so the athlete can be seen by a doctor
Nothing — scratchy feelings always go away on their own
quiz.explanation: If discomfort continues or gets worse after a simple rinse, it's time to involve an adult and get the athlete checked by a doctor — not to keep trying home remedies.

quiz.question: Why is wearing sport-specific protective eyewear a good habit in sports like hockey or racquetball?
choices: It makes players look more professional
It's more comfortable than regular glasses
It's built to help keep fast-moving balls, sticks, or fingers away from the eye
It's only needed if a player has had an eye injury before
quiz.explanation: Sport-specific eyewear is designed to protect the eye from fast-moving objects and impacts, unlike regular glasses or sunglasses. Wearing it every time, not just after an injury, is what prevents injuries in the first place.

flashcards.front: Your job when a teammate's eye gets hit
flashcards.back: Notice something is wrong and get a trusted adult right away — don't try to treat it yourself.

flashcards.front: Vision sign of a serious eye injury
flashcards.back: Blurry, doubled, or missing vision in the injured eye.
Swollen, sunken, or an odd shape compared to the other eye — get an adult even without blood.
Blood visible in the white part of the eye is a warning sign — get an adult immediately.
Never touch or try to remove it. Leave it exactly where it is and get an adult right away.

flashcards.front: Never do this to an injured eye
flashcards.back: Never rub it, never press on it, never try to force it open, never remove an object from it.

flashcards.front: Eyelid won't open after a hit
flashcards.back: A warning sign — do not force it open. Get an adult right away.

flashcards.front: Safe step for dust or an eyelash in the eye (no hit, no vision change)
flashcards.back: Blink to let tears wash it out, or have an adult gently rinse with clean water. Never rub it.

flashcards.front: When minor eye irritation needs a doctor
flashcards.back: If it still hurts or feels scratchy after blinking and rinsing, tell an adult — it needs to be checked.

flashcards.front: Why rubbing an injured eye is dangerous
flashcards.back: Even gentle rubbing or pressure can make an eye injury much worse.
Sport-specific eyewear built to keep balls, sticks, and fingers away from the eye — not the same as regular glasses or sunglasses.

flashcards.front: Best way to handle a serious eye injury
flashcards.back: Prevent it — wear the protective eyewear required for your sport every single time.
